title: ‘We are risking a $1.4 trillion boom going bust’
full_text:
Australia risks missing out on a once-in-a-generation AI investment boom worth up to $US1 trillion ($1.4 trillion) that could lift productivity and raise living standards, after American financial leaders told a Business Council of Australia delegation the next six to 12 months would be critical in the global race to attract capital.
BCA chief executive Bran Black, who led the delegation, called on the Albanese government to provide a “national statement” signalling AI and data centre investment was a priority.
Mr Black told The Australian the grouping of chief executives was repeatedly told while in the US that Australia needed to move faster, provide greater regulatory certainty, streamline project approvals and send a stronger signal that major investment was welcome. He said Australia entered the race with advantages and pointed to its democratic institutions, sound financial system, abundant natural resources and available land. But he stressed these strengths alone would not guarantee investment.
Mr Black said there was no more time for Australia to “tear ourselves up and generate this public conversation around ‘Do we want this opportunity?’ ” because harnessing the AI revolution could provide the nation with a long-term growth strategy and improve quality of life for the next generation.
“The reason why we must, as a country, be excited about this is we must lift our productivity to drive living standards, and we just aren’t,” he said. “That’s the ultimate objective … this is the chance to support Australia to pass on to its children a better quality of life.” Mr Black said the delegation kept hearing one key theme: “This could be your next iron ore boom. Don’t miss the opportunity.” “We heard that the next six to 12 months are fundamentally critical because those large technology companies are racing with one another to lay the capital foundations now – the hard physical foundations that will ultimately enable them to pursue their ambitions in AI,” he said.
“We heard of up to $US1 trillion in capital that could be deployed over the next couple of years. The key thing … was we can be part of this opportunity or not, because capital has to be deployed somewhere. We can secure it, or somebody else will. This is our window.” The business delegation was also hosted by Australia’s new ambassador to Washington, Greg Moriarty, at his residence in his first official engagement since formally taking up the post, with members expressing support for his commitment to work closely with Australian businesses.
Mr Black said primary US interest in Australia was geared around data centres but the benefits were bigger than the construction of large warehouses for computer servers and would trigger investment in construction, electricity generation, engineering, manufacturing and technologies needed to support AI systems.
“You’re not just talking about the physical centres themselves, although they are in and of themselves enormous opportunities,” he said. “It’s the construction they generate and the skill sets required for that construction.
“It’s the new power generation needed to enable these centres to work. It’s the new technologies required to facilitate better utilisation of recycled water and cooling systems. All of these things are opportunities Australia has.” He said the economic dividend would stretch from construction and engineering through to manufacturing, energy and hi-tech industries, saying Australia could position itself as a regional hub for AI infrastructure.
Mr Black also said other nations were already moving swiftly to capitalise on the AI boom by putting aggressive investment incentives in place, including “periods of no tax or guaranteed regulatory regimes so companies deploying enormous amounts of capital have confidence that the settings won’t change”.
“What we heard was that we see settings here in Australia change, and that undermines investment confidence in and of itself. We also heard that federal planning systems can be very difficult to deal with, particularly at the EPBC (Environment Protection and Biodiversity Conservation Act) level, the overlap with states and the time associated with approvals and permits. That’s not just for data centres themselves – it also spans the power generation required to facilitate their growth.” The Australian delegation included Wesfarmers CEO Rob Scott; AirTrunk founder and CEO Robin Khuda; GrainCorp managing director Robert Spurway; incoming Allens managing partner Marc Kemp; Jemena Energy lead independent director Warwick Smith and Gilbert + Tobin chairman Daniel Gilbert.
The business leaders met with senior officials from the Trump administration on Thursday and Friday in the Eisenhower Executive Office Building to discuss AI, data centres and critical minerals.
Earlier, they met some of the most senior investment banks and banking officials in New York.
Mr Black called on the Albanese government to deliver “some type of national statement to the effect this is an Australian priority, an urgent Australian priority”.
“We need a national commitment that we want to pursue this type of investment. That is the starting point in terms of sending to the world – and particularly to markets like New York – a signal that investment is welcome here,” he said. “One of the quotes that was put to us was: ‘The US says yes, Asia says yes, Europe says no. What will Australia say?’ ” Mr Black rejected suggestions AI would destroy jobs, saying discussions in the US pointed to AI completing existing roles and generating new opportunities across the economy. “I think there is broad recognition that overall this opportunity will generate more jobs than it displaces,” he said. “There are some challenges, of course, in the US with respect to planning systems, but we’ve got planning systems that are strong and robust already across Australia that help us manage these types of concerns; we think we’re well placed to address these issues.” EDITORIAL P10
